data.title: Strategy's Stretch Preferreds Offer An 11.5% Yield Paid Monthly
data.description:
Strategy's (MSTR) Variable Rate Series A Perpetual Stretch Preferreds (STRC) offer an attractive double-digit dividend yield on a monthly distribution schedule that's attractive for yield-hungry investors looking for a low-risk security to gain exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD). The risks are material. MSTR's traditional operating unit does not generate material revenue, profit, or positive cash flows. Instead, the company relies on a type of financial engineering that requires the maintenance of a positive market price to net asset value ("mNAV") to issue new common stock in perpetuity to raise liquidity needed to buy more BTC and to service the coupons and interest payments on preferreds and debt held on their balance sheet. STRC last paid out a monthly cash dividend of $0.96 per share, unchanged from the prior distribution, and $11.52 per share annualized for an 11.50% dividend yield.

The risk with investing in fixed income comes from duration and a possible suspension or default on its coupons. If I'm buying STRC, I want to know MSTR can stay current on its monthly coupon payments forever, as STRC is a perpetual security. This means there is no maturity date when MSTR will be obligated to pay back the full outstanding amount of the preferreds at their $100 per share par value. These started trading on the NASDAQ in the summer of 2025, with MSTR expanding its universe of public trading USD preferreds to four. STRC has 70,435,000 shares authorized, with 29,587,000 shares issued and outstanding as of the end of its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter.

STRC is currently the only monthly-paying preferred in this universe. The $100 per share redemption value acts as an anchor for the preferreds, with any variation limited so far, even with BTC prices down significantly from their recent peak. STRC's 52-week low of $88 per share represents a 12% drawdown from the par value, with the stock only ever trading above this value by $0.42 per share. This predictability forms part of STRC's investment pitch against the highly volatile underlying BTC that MSTR's balance sheet is built on. I last covered MSTR with a "Hold" rating in January.

mNAV, BTC Yield, and Coupon Coverage

MSTR currently holds 780,897 BTC, with a total value of $58 billion, as BTC is down 16% year-to-date to $74,354 per BTC. The BTC as a treasury company's mNAV currently stands at 1.08x. This metric captures the market price of the MSTR-to-NAV ratio, essentially highlighting the extent to which MSTR trades at a premium to its BTC holdings. The company's market valuation is at a roughly 8% premium to the value of its BTC holdings. This is critical, as it allows MSTR to maintain positive cadence with its purchases of BTC using its stock as currency. Dilution means more BTC, with the company's weighted average shares outstanding as of the end of the fourth quarter at 277,660,000, up a material 44% from its year-ago comp. MSTR views its common shares as currency for liquidity, and the positive mNAV is the backstop for the preferreds.

MSTR's BTC yield quarter-to-date currently stands at 2.3%, down sharply from a yield of 22.8% in 2025. While still positive, the figure has been trending lower on the back of an erosion of MSTR's previous tentpole premium, with the common shares down around 70% from their 52-week high. This means the company has to sell more shares to raise the same dollar amount, limiting the scope of possible BTC purchases. This stock price dip has extended to the universe of cryptocurrency companies, with MSTR still maintaining its premium but with some peers like DeFi Development (DFDV) now trading with an mNAV as low as 0.6x.

MSTR most recently purchased 13,900 BTC for $1 billion, at an average price of $71,900 per BTC. Critically, this purchase was funded by the issuance of new STRC, with around 10 million new STRC shares sold via an ongoing at-the-market ("ATM") program in April. MSTR can raise up to $21.6 billion using STRC and another $27.1 billion via continued sales of its common equity, providing a significant pathway for continued BTC purchases. The company held USD reserves of $2.25 billion as of the end of its fourth quarter, providing 21.8 months of coverage of the dividends. This helps provide support for the continued demand for the ongoing ATM.

Conclusion

I view STRC as a "Buy" on any dips below $100 per share, with the security currently trading exactly at this level, which makes it a "Hold." The dividend yield that provides 722 basis points of income over the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is too attractive to ignore, even for a business model built around selling equity to buy BTC. However, any position in STRC has to be prudently sized, with the dividend yield and monthly distributions providing a base for a high-reward and currently low-volatility security.

data.title: IGD: The Dividend Is Too Generous
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Voya Global Equity Dividend and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGD), I issued a sell rating due to the inconsistent earnings and questionable payout sustainability. Since my last coverage, the fund has done quite well and actually outperformed the S&P 500. However, I don't think this recent outperformance should mask the underlying flaws of the fund. IGD recently released an updated semi-annual report for 2026, which also prompted me to revisit the fund's internal performance, outlook, and risk profile.

At the time of my last coverage, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 5.30%. Now that the market is experiencing a bit of a recovery, IGD now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 5.18%. Despite the fund's discount to NAV valuation, this doesn't automatically mean that it is a good time to accumulate shares. For instance, IGD has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.31% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, IGD's price now trades at the higher end of its price to NAV range, which means that the fund is now relatively expensive.

The fund now offers a starting dividend yield of 10.2%, which makes it an enticing choice for income investors. However, I believe that the high yield is hurting the underlying NAV of the fund and negatively impacting the long-term performance. It appears that the distribution policy is simply too generous at the fund would benefit from a reduction. IGD includes an option writing strategy as part of its portfolio approach, which limits price growth and in favor for a high current income.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio overview, the fund has total net assets of $481M that are spread across a diverse range of equities. The fund's primary objective is to achieve a high level of income generation, while also capturing capital appreciation when possible. In order to achieve this goal, the fund invests in global common stocks that have a history of attractive dividend yields and growth potential. However, there aren't many equities that are able to support a double-digit yield so in order to get the amplified income, IGD utilizes an option writing strategy against the indices.

The included option writing strategy helps the fund harness the volatility of the market by collecting option premiums. So when markets are experiencing choppiness, this extra layer of income can serve as a buffer against losses. According to the latest fund update, IGD writes options against ~49.48% of its assets. The average life of a call option tends to average 45 days, which provides ample time for the position to participate in upside growth and generate premiums.

Market indices have been off to a rough start in 2026. Capital rotated out of technology as uncertainty across SaaS companies rise. As a result, many of the indices have continued to trend sideways. This is where IGD's strategy has proven to be effective at benefitting from the uncertainty. IGD's total return on a YTD basis sits around 5.1%, compared to the S&P 500's (SPY) total return of 0.89% and the iShares Russell 1000 Growth's (IWF) total loss of 4.3% over the same time frame. So if you believe that market uncertainty will continue, IGD may be an appropriate holding for you.

The fund doesn't try to replicate any specific index, but it does maintain global exposure across equities that management finds attractive. Approximately 67.84% of assets are allocated to U.S. focused securities, while the remaining bulk of the portfolio is spread across different regions of Asia, Europe, and Canada. Expanding on this, IGD is most heavily allocated to financials at 23.89% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrials and healthcare investments, which accounts for 13.17% and 12.04% of assets respectively.

Furthermore, the fund provides direct exposure to some of the highest quality dividend-paying companies in the world. For instance, Alphabet (GOOG) is the largest position at 2.93% of assets. This is followed by many other high quality equities, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Meta Platforms (META), AbbVie (ABBV), and Coca-Cola (KO), just to a few. The fund also provides exposure to the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), which significantly expands the reach of the fund.

Not Structurally Built For Outperformance

Since my last coverage, IGD has released an updated semi-annual report for the 2026 period. Net investment income amounted to $0.08 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.28 per share for the period. For a fund that prioritizes high distributions, I would love to see net investment income significantly improve in the future. While this may be a bit unrealistic since the fund doesn't utilize any sort of debt securities to generate income, it would substantially improve the fundamentals of the portfolio. Since net investment income is so low, the fund becomes reliant on net realized gains to drive earnings growth.

The challenge is that net realized gains are entirely dependent on IGD's ability to capture the price growth of its positions. This means that IGD has to actively sell off the upside growth of its strongest positions, which is a conflict of interest. If the fund is selling the upside growth of its own holdings, this means that its positions aren't capable of growing over time uninterrupted. It's essentially like the fund is selling off its best positions just to keep the dividend afloat.

So when looking at the performance over the last few years, we can see that IGD's share price has barely increased, despite the indices racing to all-time highs over the last year. IGD's share price has only increased by 0.5% over the last five years, while the total return including distributions jumps up to 62.1% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors are reliant on IGD's ability to maintain a high rate of payouts over time.

While I am optimistic about the growth of the market over the next few years, I don't think that IGD is a very efficient way to participate in the growth. The reality is that IGD isn't structurally built to participate in the expansion of the market when you consider the included option writing strategy and exclusion of non-dividend-paying companies. For instance, a scenario where the market expands because of the growth of AI would likely translate to underperformance for IGD. You won't find any of the large-cap leaders that don't pay a dividend, such as Amazon (AMZN), Netflix (NFLX), or Tesla (TSLA).

Furthermore, the high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to higher returns. While the fund has demonstrated its ability to outperform during periods of uncertainty, the long-term performance shows that IGD generally underperforms. IGD's total return over the last decade sits around 128.2%, which severely underperforms against SPY and IWF over the same time frame.

Dividend Is Hurting NAV Growth

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.2%. Assuming IGD keeps payouts unchanged for the next twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.60 per share. The only issue is that the fund's distribution policy is a bit too generous relative to the level of earnings the portfolio typically generates. For instance, IGD reported total earnings of $0.40 per share in 2024, as well as earnings of $0.09 per share back in 2023. While the fund is capable of generating sufficient earnings during favorable market environments, this dynamic makes it hard for the fund to provide meaningful growth.

Looking at the fund's dividend history over the last ten years, we can see that IGD's payouts have continuously shifted. As the fund's earnings fluctuate based on market conditions, management has to shift its payouts over time. However, the fund's adjusted payouts are more of a reactionary measure to poor earnings, rather than a proactive strategy that protects the NAV.

The main issue here is that the fund is paying out essentially all of its earnings to support the distribution. Furthermore, IGD frequently finds itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns. So despite market indices racing to all-time highs and IGD providing direct exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world, the underlying NAV has failed to grow over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts going back to 2020:

  • 2020: $6.26 per share
  • 2021: $6.01 per share
  • 2022: $6.36 per share
  • 2023: $5.97 per share
  • 2024: $5.89 per share
  • 2025: $6.16 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $6.22 per share

Since the starting yield is already at 10.2%, IGD could reduce its payouts and still remain competitive. For instance, a 20% reduction in payouts could translate to a starting dividend yield around 8.1%. This is still a high yield that can create a high stream of income for investors, while also improving operating margins for the fund. A lower payout would help improve the fund's NAV growth since it reduces the pressure of how much earnings IGD needs to generate.

When comparing IGD's NAV to peers in the high yield sector, we can see that the fund has failed to deliver meaningful growth. While the fund has kept its dividend yield high, it has directly impacted the overall total performance of the fund. The fund's failure to grow its NAV is one of the primary issues holding back the overall appeal.

Furthermore, we get confirmation that the fund fails to support the distributions from the latest section19(a) notice. Approximately 89% of the latest payout was classified as return of capital, which is an indication that payouts are being supported by its NAV, rather than earnings. While this form of distribution has favorable tax treatment, it also reinforces that the NAV may continue to trend downward over time if the fund isn't capable of generating sufficient earnings.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on IGD because of the structure that leads to underperformance and NAV erosion. The fund is essentially eating its own growth by selling out of high quality positions to prioritize distributions. The high yield is also a bit too generous as historical data indicates that IGD has frequently found itself in the scenario of paying out more than it earns. Despite the fund providing direct exposure to some of the highest quality growth businesses in the world, the fund's underlying NAV growth was extremely disappointing. The option writing strategy also limits the growth potential of the fund, so if markets rise due to rapid earnings growth led by AI, IGD is likely to continue underperforming.

data.title: Miller Value Partners Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Feels Like 2022 But Isn’t

The first quarter was a weak one across markets, as both equities and bonds pulled back – the S&P 500 (SPX) fell -4.35%, US investment grade bonds were off -0.05%, and high yield declined -0.50%. The market texture was reminiscent of the first quarter of 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine, sending oil prices to nearly $130/barrel before returning to more normal levels by the end of that year. The market, however, did not quickly bounce back after that quarter’s decline – rather, it kept going down, putting in a -24% decline from the start of the year to its closing low in October. Investors are naturally wondering whether the current turmoil is likely to result in a similar decline.

Indeed, economists and markets alike noted that growth is likely to slow in conjunction with the tighter oil availability and higher gas prices. The Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta reduced its first quarter estimate for real economic growth from 3.2% to 1.3% over the span of one month due largely to reduced contributions from personal consumption and residential investment. Bond investors caught a whiff of trouble starting in February, causing the two-to-ten-year yield spread, which investors watch for a forward-looking read on the economy, to flatten to 51 basis points from 73, which was a four-year high; the flattening also reversed all of the curve’s steepening progress on the heels of the Fed’s decision to start expanding its balance sheet again (see “The World’s Biggest Buyer Is Back, and People Don’t Get It”).

While the economy is likely to slow and the bottom may not be in, the rest of the machine appears to be in a more stable position today than it was in 2022. Everything was bouncing back from COVID then, as surging demand collided with pared back supply chains, pushing headline inflation to 7% prior to the oil price shock versus inflation of just 2.7% entering 2026. At around 300 basis points, high-yield spreads were remarkably similar entering each year. While spreads blew out to 578 in 2022, high-yield creditors have so far remained more optimistic this time around, despite all the worry about private credit.

One particular dynamic that we have flagged repeatedly is that big growth continues to appear more vulnerable than smaller cap value, which manifested in a big way in the 2022 pullback and so far this year too. The Russell 2000 Value Index advanced 5% in the first quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index by nearly 1500 basis points. The S&P 500 information technology sector, which compounded at an impressive 20% annualized between 2009 and 2025, declined -9.1% in the first quarter, among the worst-performing industry groups. People that care about valuation might still find the group unattractive, as it trades at nearly an 8-turn premium to the market on an EV/EBITDA basis, putting it in the 83rd percentile on EV/EBITDA premium over the past thirty years.1 High relative and absolute valuation along with rising capital intensity and a slowing growth backdrop is generally not a great starting point for sustained future outperformance.

Conversely, low expectations in capital-intensive industries appear attractive now that capital has a cost again. Between 2010 and 2020, bond yields barely exceeded the rate of inflation, providing very little opportunity cost for investing in long-duration assets. Now, industries that generate excess capital and return it to shareholders are more compelling. Many energy stocks after the rally appear to appropriately discount $70-$80 oil but are still failing to appreciate that years of underinvestment and improved capital discipline could imply sustained high returns in a world where AI is hungry for power. Financials, which had a rough first quarter, appear poised to do well in a structurally higher rate regime, with a clear path to earnings growth and compelling valuations. The dividend yield on the market is coming off a five-decade low, which we think is a good dynamic to fade, and financials offer juicy yields too.

As always, we remain the largest investors in our strategies and appreciate your partnership. We are optimistic about the future and welcome any questions or comments.

Bill Miller IV, CFA, CMT

1As of 4/10/26

This commentary reflects the views of the author as of the date above and is subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast, outlook, or opinion will be realized. This material is for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Statements containing ‘may,’ ‘could,’ ‘expect,’ ‘likely,’ or similar words are forward-looking and involve risks and uncertainties. Actual outcomes may differ materially. Information from third-party sources is believed to be reliable but is not guaranteed as to accuracy or completeness. References to specific investments are for illustrative purposes only and are not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings and positioning are subject to change. It should not be assumed that any investment in the securities referenced was or will be profitable.

Figures cited above should be verified against primary sources (e.g., Federal Reserve releases) and may reflect market estimates. Statements reflect our interpretation of public data; exact figures may vary by source and revisions.

The information presented should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any security and should not be relied upon as investment advice. It should not be assumed that any purchase or sale decisions will be profitable or will equal the performance of any security mentioned. References to specific securities are for illustrative purposes only. Portfolio composition is shown as of a point in time and is subject to change without notice.

The views expressed in this commentary reflect those of the author as of the date of the commentary. Any views are subject to change at any time based on market or other conditions, and Miller Value Partners disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Adviser believes that the content provided by third parties and/or linked content is reasonably reliable and does not contain untrue statements of material fact, or misleading information. This content may be dated and may not have been independently audited by Miller Value Partners.

Basis point is one hundredth of one percent. The S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization-weighted index of 500 widely held common stocks. The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of the smallcap value segment of the US equity universe. The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Income, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EV/EBITDA) is the enterprise multiple and is used to determine the value of a company.

Investors cannot invest directly in an index and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Hard To Make High-Conviction Central Bank Calls
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior UK & Eurozone Rates Strategist

Not an easy job to predict the next move for central banks

The ECB is clearly not set on raising policy rates this April already, but market expectations remain high for a hike in June. Currently, a full 25bp of hikes is priced in by June and at least one more hike by the end of this year. Much will depend on the oil price, however, because for every $10 increase in the oil price, the market’s hiking expectations increase by roughly 25bp. Similarly for the Fed, and especially the Bank of England, the correlation between policy expectations and oil prices remains very tight. With current volatility in oil markets, a $10 move can materialise in just one day.

The volatility in short-end rates makes it difficult for market players to take positions, potentially distorting the relationship between actual expectations and market pricing. Even if an investor has a high-conviction view of a central bank’s reaction function, taking a position is risky, as a single geopolitical headline can trigger a sharp jump in the wrong direction. These dynamics can result in lower market liquidity, wider bid-ask spreads and less price discovery. In effect, the relatively hawkish pricing of central bank reaction functions may not fully reflect market expectations.

Nevertheless, markets continue to guess the direction of policy rates and the many central speakers slated for this week can help. On Tuesday, Lagarde and the IMF emphasised the risks to growth dynamics in the current environment, which all else should temper rate hike expectations. The focus from the ECB’s president on data dependency also suggests April might be too early for a change in policy. Together with another drop in oil prices, it has helped reduce the probability of a rate cut in April to around 25%.

Wednesday’s events and market views

From the eurozone, we have industrial production data, but given the data is still from February, this shouldn’t be market-moving. From the US, we have weekly mortgage applications and the Empire Manufacturing Index. The Empire Index is from April and could give some timely insights into how the high oil prices are impacting the economy. Later in the day, we have the Fed’s Beige Book, which provides valuable qualitative commentary about the state of the economy.

In terms of central bank speakers, we have the ECB’s Lagarde, Schnabel and Villeroy. From the Fed, we have Barr and Bowman, and from the BoE, we have Bailey.

Supply includes an Italian syndication of 10Y BTPs for an estimated €15bn and a 20Y BTPei for c.€5bn. Germany will auction 22Y, 26Y and 30Y Bunds totalling €3bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Las Vegas Sands: Singapore Is Not Enough
data.description:
Summary

Las Vegas Sands (LVS) is one of the premier integrated resorts globally with a heavy presence in the core Asian gaming markets of Macau and Singapore. Our main concern with the stock is a potential slowdown in Macau gaming growth coupled with long-term margin compression from excess competition in Macau as new competitor capacity comes online in 2026-2027. While the Singapore gaming market remains robust, without significant margin and EBITDA improvement in Macau, we believe the stock has more downside from current levels.

All integrated resorts are in Asia

LVS EBITDA is derived from Singapore and Macau, as the company no longer has any assets in the United States. LVS operates Marina Bay Sands ((MBS) in Singapore and operates Sands Macau, Venetian, Londoner, Parisian, and The Plaza in Macau. LVS completed the sale of its Las Vegas resort assets in February 2022 to private equity group Apollo and VICI Properties.

Singapore > Macau in terms of EBITDA

As a percentage of adjusted property EBITDA in 2025, Singapore was roughly 56% and Macau was 44% of LVS' $5.2bn EBITDA whereas in 2023-2024, Macau was the majority of group EBITDA at roughly 54%.

Not only is Singapore a greater percentage of EBITDA, but adjusted property EBITDA margins are also higher in Singapore, with 4Q25 margins reaching 50.3% (+3.1% yoy) while Macau EBITDA margins fell to 29.5% (-2.7% yoy).

Margins are higher in Singapore as gaming tax rates are lower when compared to Macau (Singapore at 18-22% compared to Macau at 40%). In addition, Singapore only has two integrated resorts split between two gaming operators, while Macau has twenty casinos spread among six operators. As a result, gaming operators in Macau face stiffer competition to gain customers and recently have spent more in terms of marketing expenses to attract players.

Not in the right segment yet

The premium mass gaming segment has been driving the majority of the GGR growth recently in Macau. However, LVS' product offering in Macau is geared more towards the mid- to lower spending player, which has not seen a significant recovery yet. In terms of product offering, LVS is disadvantaged vs. competitors such as Wynn and MGM, who traditionally have targeted the higher end segment through more premium service along with higher end properties that cater to the more affluent player.

Intense competition in Macau

We expect the competition for player acquisition to remain intense in Macau in the near-term as new gaming capacity continues to come online. LVS' largest competitor in Macau, Galaxy Entertainment, launched the ultra-high end Capella Hotel in February. Galaxy will also launch Phase 4 of its main integrated resort in addition to completing renovation of its StarWorld property in 2027. Melco Resorts is also expected to open its new hotel REM, formerly The Countdown, in 2026. Other gaming operators such as Wynn, LVS, SJM, and MGM will mainly focus on internal refurbishments and along with continued ramp up of existing properties, compete with the new capacity.

We expect player reinvestment costs to remain high as operators with new capacity need to draw in new customers, while operators without new capacity need to reinvest in order to keep their current client base from leaving. Hence, we do not expect any sustainable increase in EBITDA margins or EBITDA in the near term for LVS. In addition, new capacity is likely to lead to market share losses for LVS, which was the case in 2025, further pressuring profit growth.

Intense competition in regionally

Longer term, not only do we see increased capacity in Macau, but regional integrated resorts are likely to come online starting in 2030 with MGM Osaka. In addition, the Japanese government will open another bidding process in 2027 for a second gaming site. Furthermore, Wynn Al Marjan Island in UAE is expected to open in 2027, coupled with new projects in Manila and Cambodia from 2026-2029.

Gaming growth slowing in Macau

Total GGR (gross gaming revenue) for Macau grew by 9% in 2024 vs. 24% in 2023. YTD through March, GGR is +14%. Current consensus estimates for GGR growth in 2026 are around mid-single digits. In 2025, Macau's GGR per visitor fell by 5% yoy. One of the main reasons is because Macau has continued to build out its non-gaming infrastructure in the forms of arenas to host concerts and convention halls for MICE events. Hence, the type of customer coming to Macau may not be the heavy gambler but now instead is more of the leisurely customer who does not gamble.

Macro issues hurt GGR

We expect gaming revenues to see slower growth rates in 2026 due to geopolitical risks leading to greater consumer uncertainty and slower economic growth from rising oil prices. In addition, prolonged economic slowdown in China, where the Chinese government forecasted GDP growth of 4.5% vs. 5% in 2025, coupled with a weak property market, slowing consumer consumption, and tariff uncertainty, are all likely to rein in consumer spending in 2026, which could further hurt gaming revenues.

Singapore is strong but not enough

MBS is seeing robust growth due to strong international tourism into Singapore, which helped to push Singapore GGR by 24% in 2025 (vs. 8% in 2024). In addition, LVS has shifted its customer focus at MBS to a more premium base, which results in greater operating leverage for earnings. In addition, LVS has captured higher GGR market share in Singapore against its main competitor, Resorts World, due to having a better product offering catering to the premium customer segment.

LVS will also build its fourth tower at MBS, which will feature a 55 story luxury building coupled with a 15,000 seat arena, MICE space, along with additional gaming and retail areas. The expected opening date is early 2031.

Despite strong Singapore growth, LVS' share price fell by as much as 13% after the company reported 4Q25 results in late January 2026 due to weakness in Macau. Hence, we believe Macau is still a major component of share price sentiment.

Lack of catalysts

We suspect that the promotional activity remains high for Macau player acquisition and hence expect more downside than upside surprise for LVS shares. LVS' quarterly dividend has recently only been around 1/3 of its pre-pandemic levels. On a forward EV/EBITDA basis, LVS is trading around 10.5-11x compared to its historical average of 13x, and while the valuation remains cheaper from a historical perspective, we would remain cautious given questionable growth prospects in Macau.

Upside risks

Upside risks to our view include stronger than expected EBITDA recovery in Macau from market share gains or lower reinvestment rates. Also, if Macau GGR surprises on the upside, the entire universe of Macau gaming stocks tends to react positively.

Conclusion

We would be sellers of the stock at these levels given LVS' lack of new Macau capacity, its premium product offering being weaker compared to peers, a potential slowdown in Macau gaming growth, coupled with both short- and long term competitor capacity expansion leading to higher marketing costs, lower market share, and earnings compression in Macau.

data.title: Albertsons: Private Labels And Data Drive The Long-Term Edge Despite Opioid Headwinds
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Albertsons Companies (ACI), I highlighted how the “Failed Kroger Deal Fallout Creates An Attractive Entry Point,” rating them a Buy thanks to their robust cash flow, digital and pharma expansion, and significant cost-savings program helping them navigate a rough environment.

Following the opioid settlement news overshadowing the strong dividend increase and renewed buybacks alongside higher investments, ACI continues to trade at an attractive valuation that already seems to take into account several risks.

Business Transformation Continues

ACI’s latest report was solid overall, with a beat on EPS despite a slight miss on revenue, reporting a 2% increase in identical sales and a strong 21% jump in digital sales in 2025, as well as a massive $773.8 million accounting loss related to the Opioid Settlement Framework they announced on the day of the release, expected to be completed over the following 9 years.

Meanwhile, the free cash flow dropped to $527.3 million in FY25 compared to $749.4 million in FY24 despite a drop in CAPEX, with a big impact coming from negative working capital change related to inventories, which doesn't indicate a major deterioration in the business.

For FY26, ACI expects identical sales to be 0% to 1% higher (150 bp headwind from the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program), with $3.85 billion to $3.925 billion in Adj. EBITDA and $2 billion to $2.2 billion in CAPEX. The latter is significantly above the ~$1.84 billion spent in FY25, backing their digital/e-commerce infrastructure and remodeling stores while also aiming to automate their supply chain, which is part of their $1.5 billion productivity savings 3-year target announced over a year ago.

Financially, based on ACI’s latest report, we continue to see an overall normal position for a company in this industry, with current assets below their current liabilities and growing but still manageable debt.

Recently, ACI also priced a $2.1 billion senior notes offering, with $1.2 billion due in 2032 coming at 5.625% and $900 million due in 2034 at 5.75%. This is used to redeem their $1.35 billion 4.625% notes due 2027 and $750 million 5.875% notes due 2028, pushing their maturities to a much more comfortable spot while also increasing their interest expenses by roughly $12.75 million per year.

During this report, Albertsons also announced a major 13.3% increase in their dividend, bringing it to $0.17 per share quarterly, for a yield of about 4% now, while also upsizing their buyback authorization to $2 billion after spending a strong $1.49 billion in FY25, for a combined $1.81 billion, meaning a combined yield of 20.9% in FY25, thanks especially to their accelerated buyback agreement for $750 million - but very strong even without it.

Iran Conflict Elevates the Risk

With mixed signals regarding the Iran conflict following the US blockade announcement after failed peace negotiations but some renewed optimism a day after thanks to renewed ceasefire talks, the risk of significant pressure on the environment rises with every extra day of significant war premiums on these core commodities.

At this point, it looks like we’re looking at much more than a “temporary” increase in pump prices, as even if we see an immediate peace today, the region’s energy infrastructure has suffered significant damage that the market may not be able to price in just yet, with Qatar even stating back over a month ago (around March 19) that the damage done to their Ras Laffan Industrial City following Iran’s retaliation could take between 3 and 5 years to repair.

This is just one piece of the puzzle, however, as we’re talking about spikes in vital commodities at a time when the global economy was starting to show signs of a recovery after the rate hikes following the pandemic, while also hurting the planting season significantly, potentially leading to significant issues in the near term.

As highlighted before, this can put the Fed and other central banks in a tough position as well, as they may be forced to pause rate cuts or even consider hiking if the inflationary wave is too big, which can put even more pressure on an already weak consumer environment and valuations across the board.

Meanwhile, although this weak consumer environment is also an advantage for companies like ACI (will detail this below), discretionary budgets can continue to be affected by such moves, especially if companies continue to pass these higher costs on to the consumer and borrowing costs rise, with the previous rate hikes resulting in significant trade-downs, high levels of stress, and even the essentials like groceries seeing softened spending intentions, based on a recent Deloitte report.

This is something that goes well beyond supply chain issues or tariffs, as a higher-for-longer rate environment can result in higher borrowing costs and more pressure on ACI following each refinancing, and we can see their $2.1 billion notes offering coming at higher rates than the ones they refinanced, although they won’t have to do it anymore for a while.

Even if ACI’s debt was significantly smaller, this can still affect the company from several points of view, as we’re talking about the potential to see smaller discretionary budgets for their consumers, potentially worsening the K-shaped economy and affecting the company’s sales.

The Retailer Advantage

Back in January, Morgan Stanley saw ACI as being the “most at risk in grocery wars” given their reliance on pharma sales for growth compared to peers, citing the impact from the Inflation Reduction Act and slowing GLP-1 sales - although the latter also translates into less pressure on retail spending.

However, the same institution recently released its latest expectations for GLP-1 adoption, expecting usage to reach roughly 15% of the American population by 2035, which can be a massive tailwind for their pharma sales.

At the same time, Deutsche Bank shared a very similar warning to mine regarding the consumer-packaged goods landscape, questioning the defensiveness of legacy brands in the face of the rapidly evolving retail environment. This also comes with a major tailwind for ACI, however, as private label brands can offer significant potential from several points of view.

First of all, this offers significant power of negotiation against legacy brands and especially smaller producers given the artificially lower shelf space, while private labels don’t need marketing and are basically almost the same as cutting out the middleman, while the company is targeting significant growth in private labels to as much as 30% of total sales, as highlighted previously, allowing them to capitalize on the consumer’s trade-downs.

Not only that, but these brands also offer differentiation from peers, as they’re not just another store where you find mostly the same products from the same legacy brands anymore, posing a transformative point for the industry as a whole.

On top of that, thanks to all the data gathered from these trade-downs while trust in their own brands rises, the company can further tailor their portfolio to offer what consumers want - especially if we think about their plans for digital expansion that can take this a step further while gathering important data - allowing them to also “capture” the consumer during a potential recovery.

Risks Reiterated

Although there are certain advantages when talking about retailers compared to legacy brands, the industry as a whole remains very competitive, with several other local or even European players wanting to take advantage of the current consumer trade-downs through their own private label brands.

Meanwhile, given the ongoing Iran conflict and especially the potential pressure it can come with, there is certainly room for the environment to get even worse, which can also hurt ACI and its peers.

Valuation

For ACI’s DCF to Equity valuation, given their CAPEX guidance and assuming a pivotal year, I’ll assume a starting FCF of $500 million in 2026, without taking into account any changes in net borrowings since the company’s balance sheet allows it, especially after the recent refinancing.

Assuming a normalization in CAPEX in FY27, I’ll assume this can jump to FY24’s $750 million thanks to their supply chain optimizations, cost savings, buybacks boosting the final per share result, and a potential macro recovery alongside their private label expansion offsetting the hit from the opioid settlements, which would be followed by a CAGR of 6.5% until FY30 (similar to the market's EPS estimates), with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 10% discount rate to demand a strong margin of safety given the environment, with 509.7 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4’FY25 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $11.01 billion, for an intrinsic value of $21.60 per share, which is above the current $16.12 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

ACI is rated Buy, backed by an attractive valuation and strong foundation that allows them to deliver very strong shareholder returns, standing to benefit from current and future trends alongside their digital expansion.

There are risks to remember given the fact that not even this industry is immune to the Iran conflict and significant competitive pressure, but the current valuation seems to already take into account significant levels of risk

data.title: Canadian Imperial Bank Of Commerce: Bullish Stubbornness Increases Valuation Risks
data.description:
Barely three months after the publication of my previous coverage, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce, or simply CIBC (CM) sustained its uptrend despite my cautious stance. Even so, I understand the market considering its robust fundamentals as well as the fact that price reactions are not always linear. In fact, all Canadian Big Five banks appear stubborn as their trends remain upward. However, I will still advise caution as valuation remains high today. Technicals are still clearly bullish, but overbuying and some bearish divergence signals may be an early warning to buyers.

Canadian Imperial Bank Of Commerce: How It Has Been Lately

Market volatility has persisted in recent months despite the efforts of the Fed and the BoC to lower inflation to their targets and boost economic activity in H2 2025. After all, stubborn inflation, higher tariffs, and oil price swings are still intense. This exposes many banks to various risks due to their high cyclicality and economic sensitivity. Even so, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce continue to shield its operations with its disciplined lending and asset management. This was seen in its most recent performance. Before I start, I’d like to highlight that all values here are in Canadian Dollars or CAD.

In Q1 2026, its interest income reached $11.8B, down by 7.2% YoY and 2.4% QoQ. The continued interest income decrease showed decreasing asset yields. This can tell us that the downward impact of the recent interest rate cuts continued. This was most evident in loans as its main interest-earning asset. This was logical since it has variable-rate loans, so lower interests meant lower yields on these loans.

On the brighter side, the policy easing cycle also lowered the interest on liabilities like deposits and debts. In fact, the interest expense decreased by 16.0% YoY and 5.7% QoQ. This easily offset the decrease in interest income. So, its net interest income still rose by 13.3% YoY and 4.3% QoQ. This may suggest that the policy easing cycle led to more profits for the company. On top of that, lower interest rates raised the demand for its financial products and services. For instance, loan volume increased because the interest rate cut lowered borrowing costs, which attracted more borrowers. As a result, it enjoyed higher commissions and fees, especially those related to advisory, credit, and security transactions. This helped cover non-interest expense. In the end, the net income margin reached 37% versus 29% YoY and QoQ.

Potential Risks

The bank must watch out for the potential impact of stubborn inflation, which may be intensified by tariff wars due to their tendency to raise domestic prices. This can squeeze the budget of many individuals and even small businesses, which can affect their repayment capacity. The Middle East tension and oil price swings can also be a risk due to their impact across multiple industries, which can fuel inflation further. As a result, the Fed or BoC may retract the policy easing cycle or even decide to raise rates if inflation goes out of control. While this means higher loan yields, delinquency and deposit run risks must be taken itnto account.

Why CIBC Is Strong Amid Macroeconomic Volatility

One of the things I like about CIBC is its disciplined lending strategies. Despite the interest rate cuts that could have led to an influx of loan demand, CIBC maintained stable loan volume. Also, it kept its exposure to consumer loans low. This is highly important to it because these can be prone to delinquencies and/or defaults as the rising prices may squeeze their finances. In fact, personal and credit card loans only comprise 12% of the total amount. Business and government loans comprise 40% of the total. In my view, this is the safest type of loans. After all, businesses and government institutions have higher repayment capacity. Lastly, residential mortgages are secured or collateralized, so loans here have manageable risks as well. But CIBC must understand that this is tied to the housing market. A bubble or a burst may affect it.

The consolation is that a substantial portion of its mortgages have fixed rates. So, the repayment amount will remain the same regardless of interest rate cuts or hikes. In Canadian mortgages, only 34% have variable rates, while the remaining 66% have fixed rates. More importantly, the bank has adequate provisions on credit losses. So, it may cover potential delinquencies, which is crucial in the current environment. This is supported by its loan-to-deposit ratio of 72%. This can be considered quite low and may tell that CIBC is slightly underutilizing its resources. But I like its conservative approach to lending since it ensures enough reserves, which may protect it in case of a deposit run.

Moreover, its asset diversification is still prudent. Like the rest of the Canadian Big Five, the percentage of loans to the total interest-earning assets is below 60%, or 58% to be exact. Investment securities take 28% of the total. Meanwhile, deposits are 3% of the total, while the remaining 11% goes to other interest-earning assets. This is like placing eggs into four different nests. With that, the bank diversifies or spreads risks. If one asset weakens, another one can offset the impact.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is now $3.10 versus $3.08 in my previous coverage. So, the new dividend growth is now 5.88%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 5.0%, but note that the risk-free rate is now 4.4%, which can have a downward impact on valuation. Its Beta is 1.00 in line with the S&P 500. With all this information, the TP or target price is $88.07. This shows that CM is still overpriced with downside risks.

Meanwhile, P/E shows that CM trades at 14.65x its EPS, which is higher than the five-year average of 11.11x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $78.24. This can be proven by its PEG. The EPS growth rate from FY 2025 to FY 2026 TTM is 14.66%. This shows that the PEG is already 1.00 (14.65/14.66) It means that CM is already fully priced. If we use the five-year CAGR, the EPS growth is 5.89%, which will raise the PEG to 2.49x and show overvaluation. But either way, the stock is already fully priced, which may lead to some downward pressures on it.

Technicals

CM’s technicals are still bullish as the price increases with higher highs and lows. The most recent low of $91.53 did not break below the previous low of $90.78. Also, the stock price still trends above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, although it has flattened a bit recently. This means that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Even so, MACD and RSI warrant some caution, although buying volume is still strong. The price high of $103.89 last February 26 increased to $104.88 today. However, the corresponding MACD and RSI highs on the same dates decreased. This means that the number of buyers that entered decreased as the price kept increasing. This also implies early bearish divergence signals. This is supported by potential stock overbuying, which may prompt profit-taking and push the price down again.

Conclusion

Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce remains fundamentally sound with its strategic loan management and asset diversification. This also protects its operations from more macroeconomic headwinds. However, valuation is already too high to take a position. Technicals adhere to it as the stock becomes overbought with early bearish divergence signals. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Visible Alpha Breakdown Of U.S. Airlines' Q1 2026 Earnings Expectations
data.description:
Delta reports Q1: Revenue strength masks margin strain as fuel costs bite

Delta Air Lines Inc. (DAL) has set the tone for the US airline's earnings season with first quarter results that highlight resilient demand supporting revenues, but rising costs eroding profitability and clouding the near-term outlook.

On the surface, Delta delivered a robust performance. Total revenue of $15.9 billion in Q1 beat Visible Alpha consensus preQ estimates of $14.5 billion. This upside was broad-based across passenger segments, with domestic and international revenues both exceeding estimates and showing stronger-than-expected growth.

The beat is consistent with what Delta Air Lines highlighted in its release: resilient travel demand, particularly in premium and international markets, continuing to support top-line momentum.

However, the operating backdrop was less constructive. Capacity (available seat mile), load factor, and traffic (revenue passenger mile) fell short of consensus expectations, highlighting that revenue outperformance was driven primarily by pricing rather than volume growth.

The more pronounced negative surprise came on costs. Fuel expenses for Q1 came in 14% above consensus expectations, swinging to 8.2% year-on-year growth versus an expected decline. This drove a significant miss at the operating income level (-20%) and flowed through to EPS, which fell short of expectations by over 10%.

Importantly, the revisions to Q2 expectations suggest that these pressures are not transitory. While revenue estimates have been revised meaningfully higher, with growth now expected at 11.8% year-on-year, this optimism is more than offset by a sharp increase in cost expectations, most notably fuel. As a result, operating income, net income, and EPS estimates have all been cut materially (by ~50%+), with growth expectations swinging into negative territory.

Delta’s postQ picture is one of stronger-for-longer revenue but weaker-for-longer margins. The carrier appears well-positioned to capitalize on sustained demand and pricing strength, but cost inflation, particularly fuel, is eroding incremental revenue gains, leading to a reset in profitability expectations.

Industry outlook: Q1 expectations for other US airlines

Q1 2026 expectations across other US airlines reinforce and broaden the trend seen in Delta’s results. Analyst expectations reflect that while the demand environment remains solid, profitability is increasingly dictated by cost control and operating leverage rather than pure revenue growth.

With United Airlines Holdings Inc. (UAL), Alaska Air Group Inc. (ALK), JetBlue Airways Corp. (JBLU), American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL), and Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV), reporting later in the month, consensus expectations point to resilient top-line growth, but with margin compression emerging as the defining theme, as strong demand and pricing power are increasingly offset by elevated fuel and operating costs, driven in part by geopolitical disruptions, most notably the Iran conflict and its impact on crude oil markets.

Demand to hold up, with disciplined capacity

On the demand side, analysts expect year-on-year capacity growth across the group to remain measured, largely in the low-single-digit range. Notably, JetBlue is expected to see a pullback in capacity with a 1.5% year-on-year decline in available seat mile (ASM), signaling a broader shift toward supply restraint to protect yields.

Traffic is expected to track slightly ahead of capacity growth, with revenue passenger miles projected to increase across most carriers, as demand remains intact, particularly in domestic and leisure segments, while the industry avoids overcapacity.

Load factors are consequently expected to edge higher, with the group average rising to 80.4%, up around 80 basis points from Q1 last year.

Revenue growth to remain pricing-led

Analysts expect continued strength in passenger revenues across US airlines, but as seen in Delta’s Q1 performance, this is being driven more by pricing than volume.

Revenue per available seat mile (RASM) growth is expected to remain positive across all carriers, while yields (revenue per passenger mile) are expected to climb, indicating that airlines are continuing to extract higher fares, supported by premium cabin demand, segmentation strategies and constrained capacity.

Costs seen rising, but not yet overwhelming in Q1

Fuel remains the key swing factor for the industry. Crude oil prices have risen meaningfully amid supply concerns linked to the conflict in the Middle East, resulting in rising jet fuel prices feeding into airline cost structures at a time when most US carriers have limited hedging exposure.

While oil prices have been contracting as of Wednesday, April 8, on the back of a ceasefire announcement in the US/Iran conflict, jet fuel prices are unlikely to adjust as quickly.

Against this backdrop, Q1 represents a period where airlines still benefit from a partial lag in fuel cost inflation. While jet fuel prices have risen meaningfully and are expected to feed into rising fuel expenses for the carriers in Q1, the full incremental impact of higher fuel costs is expected to be more visible in Q2 and beyond.

Profitability remains under pressure

The profitability outlook for early Q1 points to a more challenging earnings environment despite healthy demand, as rising costs, particularly fuel, begin to erode the benefits of sustained revenue strength.

At a carrier level, profitability remains mixed. United is expected to deliver a solid improvement in net income and earnings per share compared to Q1 last year, supported by its scale and premium exposure, while Southwest stands out as a relative bright spot, returning to profitability with positive earnings after a loss in the prior year, driven by its company-wide transformation plan finalized in late 2025.

Overall, the early read on Q1 points to an industry still benefiting from solid demand and disciplined capacity management but increasingly constrained on the cost side. Revenue growth remains healthy and largely pricing-driven, supported by stable load factors and modest traffic expansion across carriers.

However, elevated fuel costs are emerging as the key pressure point, driving margin compression and resulting in earnings misses and downward revisions to near-term profit expectations.

While top-line momentum is expected to persist in the near term, the numbers suggest that profitability will be more uneven across carriers, with performance increasingly dependent on each airline’s ability to control costs, manage exposure to fuel volatility, and sustain pricing power in a more challenging operating environment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
